sourceLocator: WHO 2022 global genomic surveillance strategy, goal and objectives section, pp. 4-9
claim: Genomic surveillance has public-health value when it is integrated into preparedness, surveillance, and response systems rather than treated as a stand-alone sequencing activity.
extractedText: WHO frames genomic surveillance as part of broader surveillance and laboratory systems and links it to quality, timely and appropriate public-health action, data sharing, connectivity, workforce, and emergency readiness.
notes: Use for the opening value-case framing and decision-use sections.
limitations: Broad strategic source; does not provide local implementation detail or cost estimates.

claim: Implementing pathogen genomics as a public-health service changes laboratory workflows, reporting, accreditation, staffing, IT infrastructure, LIMS integration, validation, and user engagement.
extractedText: Source evidence note: PHE case study sections on the Colindale WGS service, implementation planning, validation, accreditation, workforce, reporting, and public-health impact.
notes: Strong implementation example for explaining that infrastructure is an operating model, not just servers.
limitations: UK high-income public-health implementation context; adapt carefully for other settings.

claim: Infrastructure planning should separate what the system solves, how analysis is run, where it is run, how data flow, and who has access.
extractedText: Source evidence note: PHA4GE source pack frames infrastructure around five questions: responsibility, analysis method, compute location, data flow, and access.
notes: Use as the backbone for Gnomey profile logic and infrastructure sections.
limitations: Source pack is infrastructure-heavy and does not cover every governance or organism-specific question.

claim: A national genomic surveillance strategy should define priority use cases, data users, sample and metadata flows, analysis, interpretation, sharing, and return of results.
extractedText: WHO places priority use cases at the start of the genomic surveillance value chain and connects objectives, users, sampling strategy, sample and metadata collection, sequencing, analysis, interpretation, data publication, result sharing, and public-health action.
limitations: Strategic tool; needs operational detail from implementation sources.

sourceLocator: Armstrong et al. 2019, sections on bacterial foodborne illness, data integration, and NGS workflows
claim: The practical value of pathogen genomics depends on integrating higher-resolution sequence results with epidemiological data, workflow management, storage, and secure information sharing.
extractedText: Armstrong et al. describe WGS as higher-resolution than PFGE and state that pathogen genomic data need integration with epidemiological data, workflow management, storage, and secure sharing to realise their public-health value.
notes: Use to avoid presenting sequencing as sufficient on its own.
limitations: Review source; needs implementation sources for concrete local runbooks.

sourceLocator: WHO 2023 national genomic surveillance strategy support tool, section 2.2 principles
claim: Sampling strategy, representativeness, turnaround time, data quality, metadata completeness, and public-health reporting should be designed together because each affects how genomic data can be interpreted and used.
extractedText: WHO identifies multisource integration, representative sampling, quick turnaround, data quality, metadata completeness, and public-health reporting as linked strategy and data-management considerations.
notes: Use for sampling and lifecycle sections; do not infer organism-specific thresholds.
limitations: Does not provide organism-specific sampling fractions, turnaround targets, or QC thresholds.

sourceLocator: WHO 2025 pathogen genomic data-sharing platform guidance, sections 2.4 data scope and 2.7 data provenance
claim: A routine pathogen genomics service should preserve an identifier lineage from sample or isolate through metadata, sequencing, QC, workflow, interpretation, report, repository submission, and later reuse.
extractedText: WHO 2023 links data management to all surveillance-relevant data, interoperability, data quality, metadata quality, reporting, and public-health action. WHO 2025 defines minimum metadata and provenance across the sequence and metadata generation continuum. PHE shows LIMS, sequence analysis, quality, storage, access, and reports operating together in a service. Armstrong et al. state that pathogen genomic and epidemiological data need to be integrated to realise their value.
notes: Use as the central source-backed claim for the metadata/identifier lineage section.
limitations: Supports a general lineage model; field-level organism-specific metadata requirements still need extraction from repository and pathogen-specific sources.

sourceLocator: WHO 2025 pathogen genomic data-sharing platform guidance, sections 2.4 data scope, 2.7 data provenance, and 2.9 interoperability
claim: Repository and platform choices should be assessed against metadata scope, provenance, identifiers, interoperability, access, curation, and onward use, not only whether sequence files can be uploaded.
extractedText: WHO's platform guidance defines minimum metadata, provenance across the data-generation continuum, identifiers linking records across resources, and documented APIs and standards for exchange with other systems.
notes: Use for resource finder, data-sharing, repository, and API sections.
limitations: Platform guidance does not decide which repository is appropriate for every pathogen, jurisdiction, or sensitivity level.

sourceLocator: Gador-Whyte et al. 2026 quality management section; APHL 2016 Chapter 4; PHE case study section 3.4; WHO 2023 section 2.4
claim: A genomics service should validate and monitor the whole chain, including input material, library preparation, sequencing, QC, bioinformatics, interpretation, reporting, and external quality assessment where available.
extractedText: PHE validated before switching off conventional testing, using parallel methods, gap analysis, protocol adjustment, quality parameters and input/output assessment. WHO calls for SOPs, data-quality control, internal and external quality assessment for genomics and analytics, metadata quality, and internal consistency. APHL and the clinical microbiology review both place validation across wet-lab and bioinformatics steps.
notes: Use to replace narrow pipeline-only language in quality sections.
limitations: Detailed acceptance thresholds remain organism-specific and require separate extraction.

sourceLocator: WHO 2025 platform guidance sections 2.6 and 2.11; Gador-Whyte et al. 2026 quality management section
claim: QC status, QC methods, workflow versions, software versions, database versions, and acceptance thresholds should be recorded because low-quality data, changing workflows, and bioinformatics variation can affect interpretation and reuse.
extractedText: WHO's platform guidance supports QC annotations and version-controlled analytical tools. The clinical microbiology review states that genomic workflows require software and database version control, wet-lab and dry-lab validation, and local acceptance thresholds where standardisation is limited.
notes: Use for change-control, failure-handling, and provenance sections.
limitations: Does not yet specify a complete release-management procedure or emergency rollback process.

sourceLocator: WHO 2022 guiding principles; WHO 2023 national strategy support tool; WHO 2025 platform attributes and principles
claim: A pathogen genomics programme should define governed sharing routes for operational sharing, national databases, and public or controlled repositories, including metadata, timing, access, licences, attribution, and benefit-sharing expectations.
extractedText: WHO 2022 frames pathogen genome data sharing as a public-health activity that should be timely, ethical, equitable, efficient and effective. WHO 2023 calls for a national pathogen genome data-sharing policy and potential national databases linked to public sequence databases. WHO 2025 describes governance, access models, licences, user responsibilities, provenance, interoperability, and benefit-sharing as platform attributes.
notes: Use to avoid presenting data sharing as a single upload step.
limitations: Does not replace local legal, ethical, data-protection or international-access review.

sourceLocator: Price et al. 2023, abstract and discussion of systematic review of WGS economic evaluations
claim: Published economic evaluations generally support bacterial WGS surveillance on economic grounds, but the evidence is heterogeneous and stronger in high-income hospital and foodborne-surveillance settings than in low- and middle-income implementation contexts.
extractedText: Price et al. included 9 studies after screening 681 records and 49 full texts. All included studies supported WGS surveillance on economic grounds, but the evidence was heterogeneous and limited for low- and middle-income country implementation and real-world effectiveness.
limitations: Does not justify a universal return-on-investment claim.

claim: Public-health bioinformatics implementation needs standardized, reproducible, validated, auditable, scalable, portable, and secure analysis within laboratory operating constraints.
extractedText: Source evidence note: Microbial Genomics implementation collection includes a bioinformatics paper on standardized, reproducible, validated, auditable, scalable, portable, and secure analysis.
limitations: Needs conversion into concrete checklists and examples.
